item.title: Iran strikes vessel in Strait of Hormuz amid debate over "transit fees"
item.description: The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps struck a cargo ship near Oman on Thursday, further complicating the Iran-U.S. negotiations.

item.title: Why the Federal Reserve may raise interest rates this year
item.description: The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge climbed to 4.1% in May, its highest level in more than three years. Greg Robb, economics editor at MarketWatch, joins with analysis.

item.title: Nonprofit lawn-mowing organization hopes to mow the White House lawn for America 250
item.description: A decade ago, Rodney Smith Jr. noticed an elderly neighbor struggling to cut the grass. After stopping to help, he founded the Alabama nonprofit Raising Men & Women Lawn Care Service. Now, he's hoping to honor the president for America's 250th birthday. Tony Dokoupil has the story.

item.title: The 2026 FIFA World Cup schedule and how to watch
item.description: With 104 World Cup games being played in the U.S., Canada and Mexico, it's like "a Super Bowl every single day for five weeks," U.S. team captain Tim Ream told CBS News.

item.title: Supreme Court sides with Monsanto in case over cancer risks from weedkiller
item.description: The Supreme Court ruled that Monsanto cannot be held liable under state laws for failing to warn consumers about the alleged cancer risks of its weedkiller Roundup on its label.

item.title: U.S. playing for pride, momentum, a potential record in World Cup match against Turkey
item.description: The U.S. Men's National Soccer Team is facing Turkey in a match that, no matter the outcome, will be their last before moving on to the next round. But the stakes are still high, as Nicole Valdes reports, including pride, momentum and a potential record.

item.title: Supreme Court lets Trump strip deportation protections from Syrians, Haitians
item.description: The Supreme Court on Thursday said the Trump administration can move forward with its efforts to strip more than 356,000 Syrian and Haitian immigrants of temporary protections.

item.title: Trump's push for SAVE Act drives Congress into standoff
item.description: Lawmakers canceled Friday's votes after President Trump refused to sign a bipartisan affordable housing bill until Congress passes a package of voter regulations known as the SAVE Act. Politico congressional reporter Mia McCarthy joins to discuss.

item.title: Trump has testy meeting with GOP senators, telling Cassidy to sit down
item.description: President Trump met with Republican senators soon after canceling plans to sign bipartisan housing affordability legislation at the Capitol.

item.title: At least 30 aftershocks hit Venezuela after powerful earthquakes
item.description: At least 30 aftershocks have struck Venezuela since two powerful earthquakes rattled the country on Wednesday. CBS News meteorologist Darren Peck has more.

item.title: Supreme Court issues two big wins for Trump's immigration policy
item.description: The Supreme Court handed down rulings in a pair of immigration cases on Thursday. Jan Crawford is following the decisions.

item.title: On Trump shouting match, Sen. Bill Cassidy says, "Unfortunately, I raised my volume to match his"
item.description: Louisiana Republican Sen. Bill Cassidy got into a shouting match with President Trump in a private meeting on Wednesday, telling him the administration has not been honest with the American people about the conflict. Cassidy spoke with Margaret Brennan and Ed O'Keefe reports.

item.title: Detroit Lions player arrested on charges of armed robbery and kidnapping
item.description: Terrion Arnold, a 23-year-old defensive player for the Detroit Lions and a former first-round pick and all-American at the University of Alabama, has been arrested on charges of armed robbery and kidnapping. He is denying it all. Tom Hanson reports.

item.title: Heat waves push more Europeans to embrace air conditioning
item.description: Only 20% European homes have AC, compared to 90% in the U.S., but as the climate changes, that vast gulf may be set to shrink.

item.title: As heat wave smothers Europe, some hospital rooms lack AC
item.description: A heat wave has been smothering Europe for almost a week, smashing records, shuttering schools and claiming hundreds of lives tied to exposure and drownings. Chris Livesay reports from Paris.

item.title: Luigi Mangione's attorneys accuse federal prosecutors of violating his rights
item.description: Mangione​ is facing both state and federal charges for UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson's murder in December 2024. He has pleaded not guilty in both cases.

item.title: Powerful earthquakes strike Venezuela, hundreds dead or injured
item.description: Venezuela's acting president said the death toll from powerful twin earthquakes was likely to rise, as USGS modeling suggested thousands may have been killed.

item.title: Luigi Mangione's attorneys call report of possible plea deal "troubling"
item.description: Attorneys for Luigi Mangione are accusing prosecutors of trying to manipulate public opinion against him, and calling a report of a possible plea deal "troubling." CBS News' Anna Schecter has the latest.

item.title: DeSantis confirms "Alligator Alcatraz" shutting down operations
item.description: Conditions at "Alligator Alcatraz" were criticized by lawyers, families and human rights groups who claimed detainees were mistreated.

item.title: Video captures moment of Venezuela earthquake
item.description: Thousands are feared dead in the rubble of a devastated Venezuela on Thursday as rescue teams claw through the aftermath of two earthquakes. Lilia Luciano reports, and Rob Marciano looks into what caused the double-earthquake.

item.title: Jury remains deadlocked in trial of Palisades Fire arson suspect
item.description: A jury remains deadlocked in the trial of Jonathan Rinderknecht, the man accused of starting the blaze that turned into the deadly and destructive Palisades Fire in 2025.

item.title: Judge orders DOJ to either unredact more Epstein files or explain why it can't
item.description: A judge on Thursday ordered the Justice Department to either release unredacted versions of several files on the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein or explain why it can't do so.

item.title: Just 2 votes separate candidates in mayoral race: "Every vote matters"
item.description: Christine Erickson is two votes ahead of Jamie Smith, the Minnehaha County Auditors and Elections Office said.

item.title: Taylor Swift rents Madison Square Garden, reigniting wedding venue rumors, New York Times reports
item.description: The New York Times is reporting that Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce might tie the knot at Madison Square Garden. She has reportedly booked out the arena for the Fourth of July weekend, the rumored wedding date.

item.title: What's missing from the Epstein files? Here's what we found
item.description: The Justice Department says it's released "every document required by the Epstein Files Transparency Act," but CBS News has identified numerous gaps.

item.title: Why Christopher Nolan shot "The Odyssey" on IMAX film
item.description: Correspondent Scott Pelley and director Christopher Nolan visited FotoKem, the last motion picture lab in the world that makes 70mm prints, to see finishing touches being made to "The Odyssey," the first feature shot entirely on IMAX film.

item.title: Sealand: The micronation carving its own path
item.description: This week on 60 Minutes, Jon Wertheim reported on Sealand, the tiny principality fueled by humor and determination. But first he had to get there.

item.title: Anderson Cooper on witnessing the monarch butterfly migration
item.description: The annual migration of monarch butterflies is one of science's great mysteries: millions of monarchs know the correct path even though they have never made the long journey themselves. As 60 Minutes found, the expedition to watch the migration can be its own challenge.

item.title: Capturing the emotional journey of Healing Justice
item.description: Lesley Stahl and 60 Minutes producer Shari Finkelstein took a unique approach to their story, capturing the raw emotions of crime victims and wrongfully convicted men during a three-day retreat.

item.title: Eyewitness: How accurate is visual memory? | 60 Minutes Archive
item.description: In 2009, Lesley Stahl reported on flaws in eyewitness identification procedures that led to the wrongful conviction of people like Ronald Cotton, who was later exonerated by DNA evidence for the rape of Jennifer Thompson.
